story:
Conrad is the first to go, split mid-sentence, his body peeled open as if by invisible razor wire. The weapon isn’t rushing; it’s showing off. It could wipe us all at once, but it prefers the hunt. That’s supposed to be good news. If it follows its design, we just have to do ours: stay down, stay ahead, stay alive.

I hit the dirt, roll, run low. Two more men fold in front of me. One is Josh Sasenick—twenty-three, a volunteer, too young to know better. The woods fill with gunfire and dying. They didn’t know what they’d signed up for. I did. I’m their CO. That stain belongs to me.

The weapon is a multi-agent system: a pack-mind made of ultra-strong, semi-rigid nano-film that can take any shape it wants. Its brain is distributed across its pieces—robust, adaptive, evolving. Every kill makes it smarter. We told the team most of this. We also said it was loose on the island, no longer under control, and that we were the only line between it and whatever comes next. Naturally, there’s always a kill switch. The only question is: who holds it?

An hour later, just me and Jax remain. Her night vision dies; the comms crackle even though they swore the radios were shielded. She hears the machine and opens up with her M17. I watch her through my visor feed; her rounds might as well be rain. The weapon hangs above her, studying its work. A net blooms from its body, edges singing with a thin metallic whine. She turns, clutching something small and dark. The cameras—yes, there are cameras—zoom in. It’s over in a blink.

Because this is a show. Far above us, lenses whir and focus, catching every angle, every trick of the kill. Buyers watch and bid: NATO and friends, for now. The weapon performs. We die. Someone profits.

I stand and clap, stepping past what’s left of a man—just a leg—and wave my little black kill switch. I’ve played my part. Like Sun Tzu says, all warfare is deception. Command chose me because I’d be smart, because I’d wait. I’d let the machine do its dance, then end it with one press and walk away the clever survivor. That was the plan.

The machine lifts above the tree line, compact as a football, then grows as it glides toward me. I smile, press the button. Nothing. I press again. Nothing. Again and again—still nothing. Static answers my Mayday. I look from the approaching weapon to the useless button in my hand.

Then I replay Jax’s last seconds and zoom on her fingers. The same switch is in her palm. I glance at a shredded pocket on a dead man’s fatigues. Another switch, identical. Not control—props. Not for the weapon. For us.

I drop mine. My visor shows me my own face—panic frozen under the helmet. I rip it off and finally see with my own eyes as the weapon poises above me and begins to fall, almost graceful. Somewhere, a faceless buyer presses the real kill switch—one meant not to stop the machine, but to finish the demonstration, with me.

The weapon did what it was designed to do. So did I. Only the kill switch was never in my hand.
